rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 18, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1. 1. Defendant Cahill's Motion for Summary Judgment, or, in the alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Opposition filed. Reply filed. Plaintiffs' objection to Defense Exhibit M, South African Death Certificate of Decedent, is OVERRULED. California Evidence Code section 1281 makes death certificates admissible and exempt from the hearsay rule. As Plaintiffs point out, however, the bare notation in a foreign death certificate that Decedent died of "natural causes" is of limited if any probative value, and certainly cannot overcome contrary medical evidence. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit A, Declaration of Richard Cohen, M.D., M.P.H. 74-76 [opining that Decedent's malignant pleural mesothelioma and asbestosis each contributed to his death].) Defendant appropriately abandons the general causation ground for its motion in its reply brief. (Reply, 1.) Plaintiffs' complaint alleges that Stanford Hardeman's ("Decedent") mesothelioma was a result of his exposure to asbestos and asbestos containing products used or supplied by the actions of the Defendants, including Defendant Cahill Contractors, Inc ("Defendant") when he worked as a sprinkler fitter from 1964 to 1974. Defendant argues Plaintiffs have no evidence that Decedent worked with or around Defendant's products or employees or that any such alleged exposure was a substantial factor in causing his mesothelioma. Defendant also seeks summary adjudication as to the claim for punitive damages. Defendant did not demonstrate by way of material facts included in the separate statement that Plaintiffs do not have, and cannot reasonably obtain, evidence of causation. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1442-1443; Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal. App. 4th 96, 105.) A motion for summary judgment must be accompanied by a "separate statement setting forth plainly and concisely all material facts that the moving party contends are undisputed." (Code Civ. Proc., Section 437c, subd. (b)(1).) Each of the material facts in the separate statement must be "followed by a reference to the supporting evidence." (Id.) In particular, the citation to the evidence in support of each material fact must include reference to the exhibit, title, page, and line numbers. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1350(d)(3).) A moving party's failure to comply with the procedural requirements for a separate statement may justify the denial of a summary judgment motion. (Code Civ. Proc., Section 437c, subd. (b)(1).) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 18, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1. 2. Defendant J.S Guerin & Company Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed. Reply filed. Plaintiffs' objection to Defense Exhibit M, South African Death Certificate of Decedent, is OVERRULED. California Evidence Code section 1281 makes death certificates admissible and exempt from the hearsay rule. Plaintiffs' complaint alleges that Stanford Hardeman's ("Decedent") mesothelioma was a result of his exposure to asbestos and asbestos containing products used or supplied by the actions of the Defendants, including Defendant J.S. Guerin & Company, Inc. ("Defendant") when he worked as a sprinkler fitter from 1964 to 1974. Specifically, Plaintiffs allege Defendant was a supplier of asbestos containing material, including joint compound, that was used in Decedent's presence in a way that created airborne dust which he inhaled, and which contributed to cause his mesothelioma and death. "[S]ummary judgment law in this state [no] longer require[s] a defendant moving for summary judgment to conclusively negate an element of the plaintiff's cause of action." (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal. 4th 826, 853.) "The defendant may also present evidence that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence." (Id. at 855.) "Circumstantial evidence supporting a defendant's summary judgment motion can consist of 'factually devoid' discovery responses from which an absence of evidence can be inferred, but the burden should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Ganoe v. Metalclad Insulation Corp. (2004) 227 Cal.App.4th 1577, 1582 (cleaned up).) In asbestos cases, a defendant's initial burden on summary judgment may be met when a plaintiff's discovery responses provide "little more than general allegations against the defendant and did not state specific facts showing that the plaintiff was actually exposed to asbestos-containing material from the defendant's products." (Id. at 1583 (cleaned up).) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 18, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1. 6. Defendant Fairfax Building Supply Co., Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the alternative, Summary Adjudication is OFF CALENDAR. Opposition filed. Plaintiffs' complaint alleges that Stanford Hardman's ("Decedent") mesothelioma was a result of his exposure to asbestos products which were manufactured, supplied, disturbed, used, or distributed by Defendants, including Defendant Fairfax Building Supply Co., Inc ("Defendant") when he conducted home renovations. Plaintiffs argue that Defendant's motion is fatally defective because they did not receive sufficient notice of the motion as required under Code of Civil Procedure sections 437c(a) [pre- January 1, 2025 amendment] and 1010.6(a)(4)(B).) Plaintiffs contend that Defendant's motion is untimely served because Defendant failed to provide Plaintiffs with the requisite two additional court days for e-service. The Court agrees. Code of Civil Procedure Section 437c(a)(2) provides that "notice of the motion and supporting papers shall be served on all other parties to the action at least 75 days before the time appointed for hearing." If the notice is served electronically, the required 75-day period of notice must be increased by two court days. (Code Civ. Proc., Sections 437c(a)(2) and 1010.6(a)(4)(B); see Cole v. Superior Court (2022) 87 Cal.App.5th 84, 87-88.) The Court cannot shorten the notice period without the parties' prior consent. (Urshan v. Musicians' Credit Union (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 758, 764; McMahon v. Super. Ct. (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 112, 117-118.) The Court cannot cure the defect by continuing the hearing for the missing number of days. (Robinson v. Woods (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1258, 1267-1268.) Here, Defendant's proof of service reflects that Defendant delivered its notice of motion by electronic service and filing on Thursday, December 5, 2024. However, the last day for such method of delivery for hearing on February 18, 2025 was Tuesday, December 3, 2024. Based on the foregoing, the Court agrees with Plaintiffs that the notice of the motion was not timely served. Furthermore, the Court cannot cure the defect by continuing the motion to a later hearing date. Accordingly, Defendant's motion for summary judgment, or in the alternative, summary adjudication is off calendar. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on Friday, February 14, 2025, and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on February 18, 2025, at 9:00 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. =(304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 18, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1. 4. Defendant Pierce Lathing Co's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed. Reply filed. Plaintiffs' objection to Defense Exhibit M, South African Death Certificate of Decedent, is OVERRULED. California Evidence Code section 1281 makes death certificates admissible and exempt from the hearsay rule. Plaintiffs' complaint alleges that Stanford Hardeman's ("Decedent") mesothelioma was a result of his exposure to asbestos and asbestos containing products used or supplied by the actions of the Defendants, including Defendant Pierce Lathing Co. ("Defendant") when he worked as a sprinkler fitter from 1964 to 1974. Specifically, Plaintiffs allege Defendant was a contractor that applied and disturbed asbestos-containing fireproofing in Decedent's presence in a way that created airborne dust which he inhaled, and which contributed to cause his mesothelioma and death. "[S]ummary judgment law in this state [no] longer require[s] a defendant moving for summary judgment to conclusively negate an element of the plaintiff's cause of action." (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal. 4th 826, 853.) "The defendant may also present evidence that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence." (Id. at 855.) "Circumstantial evidence supporting a defendant's summary judgment motion can consist of 'factually devoid' discovery responses from which an absence of evidence can be inferred, but the burden should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Ganoe v. Metalclad Insulation Corp. (2004) 227 Cal.App.4th 1577, 1582 (cleaned up).) In asbestos cases, a defendant's initial burden on summary judgment may be met when a plaintiff's discovery responses provide "little more than general allegations against the defendant and did not state specific facts showing that the plaintiff was actually exposed to asbestos-containing material from the defendant's products." (Id. at 1583 (cleaned up).) Defendant contends that Plaintiffs do not have and cannot reasonably obtain evidence to show Decedent was exposed to asbestos from products supplied by Defendant. Defendant argues that Plaintiffs' responses to interrogatories are sufficient for Defendant to meet its burden in moving for summary judgment, which shifts the burden of proof to Plaintiffs to prevent evidence creating a triable issue with regard to exposure. (Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal. 4th at 851.) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, February 18, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1. 5. Defendant Anderson, Rowe, & Buckley, Inc's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed. Reply filed. Plaintiffs' objection to Defense Exhibit I, Deposition of Richard Buckley, on the ground that it is hearsay is SUSTAINED. Defendant has not satisfied the prerequisites to the exception to the hearsay rule for prior testimony. (Evid. Code Sections 1290-1292.) Plaintiffs' objection to Defense Exhibits E, F, G, Plaintiffs' responses to Special Interrogatories, is OVERULED in part and SUSTAINED in part. The objection is SUSTAINED as to the responses to Special Interrogatory No.1, as the excerpt attached to Defendant's moving papers is incomplete. However, the objections to the responses to Special Interrogatories No. 2-3 are OVERRULED, as the responses address the issues presented by Defendant. "[S]ummary judgment law in this state [no] longer require[s] a defendant moving for summary judgment to conclusively negate an element of the plaintiff's cause of action." (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal. 4th 826, 853.) "The defendant may also present evidence that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence." (Id. at 855.) "Circumstantial evidence supporting a defendant's summary judgment motion can consist of 'factually devoid' discovery responses from which an absence of evidence can be inferred, but the burden should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Ganoe v. Metalclad Insulation Corp. (2004) 227 Cal.App.4th 1577, 1582 (cleaned up).) In asbestos cases, a defendant's initial burden on summary judgment may be met when a plaintiff's discovery responses provide "little more than general allegations against the defendant and did not state specific facts showing that the plaintiff was actually exposed to asbestos-containing material from the defendant's products." (Id. at 1583 (cleaned up).) Defendant contends that Plaintiffs do not have and cannot reasonably obtain evidence to show Decedent was exposed to asbestos from products distributed, removed, or replaced by Defendant. Defendant relies on Plaintiffs' responses to interrogatories and on Decedent's brother Mark Hardeman's testimony. Defendant argues Mr. Hardeman's testimony shows that he could not identify any other mechanical contractors at any jobsite that he and Decedent worked and that it would be unusual for another mechanical contractor to have been present. (Defense Exhibit H at 384:5-21.) Overall, Defendant argues that Plaintiffs' responses to interrogatories and Mr. Hardeman's deposition testimony is sufficient for Defendant to meet its burden in moving for summary judgment, which shifts the burden of proof to Plaintiffs to prevent evidence creating a triable issue with regard to exposure. (Aguilar, Supra, 25 Cal. 4th at 851.) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT PIERCE LATHING CO. (DBA PIERCE ENTERPRISES) MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Plaintiffs contend that Defendant has not established that Plaintiffs' discovery responses, taken as whole, including product identification witnesses and deposition testimonies of two of Decedent's deceased coworkers, are devoid of facts suggesting that Plaintiffs may be able to prove that Decedent worked with and around Defendant's asbestos-containing products. (Opposition, at p. 4.) The Court agrees. Here, Defendant asserts that the only witnesses Plaintiffs identified in response to Defendant's Special Interrogatories are Plaintiffs themselves, Mark Hardeman, and Thomas McCollum. (UMF No. 8.) Defendant argues Plaintiffs failed to indicate where Mr. McCollum had information relevant to the claim against Defendant. (UMF No. 9; Motion, at p. 2.) However, as Plaintiffs note in their opposition, Plaintiffs' response stated that Decedent was exposed to asbestos-containing fireproofing and stucco products supplied by Defendant at various jobsites, four of which were identified, between 1967 and 1973. The response further identified Decedent's coworkers, including Mr. McCollum, and identified him as a witness with knowledge of facts, including that he recalled Defendant's employees working at two specific job sites installing asbestos-containing fireproofing when Decedent was working there. (Opposition, at p. 9; Defense Exhibit G at 5:9-13.) Plaintiffs also identified the transcripts of two of Decedent's deceased coworkers, George Humphries and Allen Kulikoff, and stated that they similarly testified that they worked at two different job sites installing asbestos-containing fireproofing and plastering products at times when Decedent was working there. (Defense Exhibit G at 5:15-24.) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 3 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT J.S. GUERIN & COMPANY, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Defendant contends that Plaintiffs do not have and cannot reasonably obtain evidence to show Decedent was exposed to asbestos from products supplied by Defendant. Overall, Defendant argues that Plaintiffs' responses to interrogatories are sufficient for Defendant to meet its burden in moving for summary judgment, which shifts the burden of proof to Plaintiffs to prevent evidence creating a triable issue with regard to exposure. (Aguilar, 25 Cal. 4th at 851.) Plaintiffs contend that Defendant has not established that Plaintiffs' discovery responses, taken as whole, including identified product identification witnesses and deposition testimony by two of Decedent's deceased coworkers, are devoid of facts suggesting that Plaintiffs may be able to prove that Decedent worked with and around Defendant's asbestos-containing products. (Opposition, at p. 4.) The Court agrees. Here, Defendant asserts that the only witnesses Plaintiffs identified in response to Defendant's Special Interrogatories are Plaintiffs themselves, Mark Hardeman, and Thomas McCollum. (UMF No. 8.) Defendant argues Plaintiffs failed to indicate where Mr. McCollum had information relevant to the claim against Defendant. (UMF. No. 9; Motion, at p. 2.) However, as Plaintiffs note in their opposition, Plaintiffs' response stated that Decedent was exposed to asbestos containing drywall products/joint compound supplied by Defendant at various jobsites, four of which were identified, between 1967 and 1973. The response further identified Decedent's coworkers, including Mr. McCollum, and identified him as a witness with knowledge of facts, including that he saw Defendant's trucks unloading joint and tape compounds at a job site in 1971, during the period of time when Decedent worked there. (Opposition, at p. 9; Defense Exhibit G at 4:1-8:10.) Plaintiffs also identified the transcripts of two of Decedent's deceased coworkers, Gilbert Fontana and Robert Cantley, and stated that they similarly testified that Defendant's trucks delivered joint compounds and other products at two different job sites at a time when Decedent was working there. (Defense Exhibit G at 5:8-14; 7:26-8:11; 8:22-9:10.) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 3 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT J.S. GUERIN & COMPANY, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 3 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Thus, unlike Andrews v. Foster-Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, Plaintiffs' interrogatory responses do provide specific facts known to Plaintiffs regarding Decedent's exposure to Defendant's products at specific jobsites. As a result, Defendant did not carry its initial burden on summary judgment. (See Ganoe, 227 Cal.App.4th at 1584-1585 [reversing summary judgment for defendant where plaintiffs' amended discovery responses cited to specific facts linking defendant to the decedent's exposure to asbestos, including identifying decedent's coworker as a product identification witness].) Defendant does not address any of that evidence, and makes no showing that it deposed or attempted to depose Mr. McCollum. "A motion for summary judgment is not a mechanism for rewarding limited discovery; it is a mechanism allowing the early disposition of cases where there is no reason to believe that a party will be able to prove its case." (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1442 [reversing summary judgment where defendant "produced no evidence allowing an inference that plaintiffs neither possess, nor reasonably can obtain, any evidence that [decedent] was exposed to an asbestos-containing product manufactured or supplied by [defendant]"]; see also Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 ["[c]ourts can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered"].) In the absence of evidence that Mr. McCollum will be unable to testify that Decedent was exposed to asbestos-containing products supplied by Defendant, the Court cannot find that Plaintiffs will be unable to establish causation as to Defendant. In addition, Plaintiffs also identified Defendant itself as a product identification witness, and Defendant failed to address the knowledge it possesses. (Defense Exhibit G at 7:26-8:11.) Defendant therefore failed to shift the burden to Plaintiffs, and its motion for summary judgment must be denied on that ground alone. Defendant also contends that according to Decedent's death certificate he died in South Africa of "natural causes." (Motion, at p. 1.) As the opposition notes, Defendant seems to be arguing that Decedent did not have an asbestos caused death because his death certificate states that he died of natural causes. (Opposition, p. 9.) However, Defendant's motion offers no argument on this issue and instead focuses on the claims of exposure and product identification. Regardless, Plaintiffs filed an expert declaration from Dr. Richard Cohen, in which he opines that the Decedent's death was contributed to by his two separate asbestos-caused diseases, asbestos and mesothelioma. (Plaintiff Exhibit A at 1-80.) Thus, Defendant fails to foreclose the possibility that Plaintiffs may yet prove asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant. (See Strobel v. Johnson & Johnson (2021) 70 Cal.App.5th 796, 802-803, 811-828.) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 4 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT J.S. GUERIN & COMPANY, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 4 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Finally, although Defendant's motion is brought on the alternative ground that "Decedent's employer engaged in superseding conduct that intervenes in the chain of causation and destroys the element of causation as to Defendant" (Motion, 2), Defendant presents no evidence or argument on that issue in its moving papers, and therefore must be deemed to have abandoned it. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on Friday, February 14, 2025, and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on February 18, 2025, at 9:00 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. =(304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT CAHILL CONTRACTORS, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 3 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) On reply, Defendant belatedly attaches the correct version of Plaintiff's responses to Special Interrogatories. (Defense Exhibit A.) Defendant argues that attaching the wrong version in Exhibit G was an inadvertent mistake. (Reply, at p. 2.) Defendant argues that Plaintiffs were aware of these responses, knew what the basis for the Motion was, and had full opportunity to respond. (Id.) Defendant contends that there are "numerous written opinions in California that state missing exhibits or errors in attaching exhibits are not by itself sufficient bases to deny written motions." (Id.) However, neither of the two cases Defendant cites stands for the proposition that a court may excuse missing evidence on a motion for summary judgment. To the contrary, Code of Civil Procedure section 437c(b)(1) requires the moving party to submit admissible evidence with the motion. If evidence is missing from the motion, the moving party has failed to meet its initial burden, and the motion may be denied on that basis. Moreover, a moving party on a motion for summary judgment may not rely upon evidence filed for the first time within its reply papers. "The general rule of motion practice, which applies here, is that new evidence is not permitted with reply papers. This principle is most prominent in the context of summary judgment motions, which is not surprising, given that it is a common evidentiary motion. The inclusion of additional evidentiary matter with the reply should only be allowed in the exceptional case and if permitted, the other party should be given the opportunity to respond." (Jay v. Mahaffey (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 1522, 1537 (cleaned up); see also San Diego Watercrafts, Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308, 313 ["While the [Code of Civil Procedure] provides for reply papers, it makes no allowance for submitting additional evidence [therein]"].) If the Court were to consider the evidence submitted for the first time within Defendant's reply papers, this Court would violate Plaintiff's due process rights as Plaintiffs did not have notice or the opportunity to address the evidence upon which Defendant's argument is based. (San Diego Watercrafts, 102 Cal.App.4th at 316 ["Here, the evidence not only was omitted from the separate statement, it also was not filed until after [plaintiff] had responded to the issues raised in the separate statement. In considering this evidence, the [trial] court violated [plaintiff's] due process rights. [Plaintiff] was not informed what issues it was to meet in order to oppose the motion"].) Therefore, the Court finds that Defendant has failed to shift the burden, thus the motion must be denied. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 4 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT ANDERSON, ROWE & BUCKLEY, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND NOTICE OF MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 3 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Lastly, Defendant points to Decedent's brother Mark Hardeman's deposition testimony to show that he could not identify any other mechanical contractors at any jobsite where he worked with Decedent. Asked whether there were other mechanical contractors at those jobsites, Mr. Hardeman responded that he "wouldn't be able to say, one way or the other," but that it would be "unusual." (Defense Exhibit H, at 384:5-21.) However, Defendant failed to demonstrate he lacks information regarding Defendant's work around Decedent. During his deposition, Defendant's counsel did not ask Mr. Hardeman about Defendant, or if he ever saw Defendant perform work around Decedent. Instead, Mr. Hardeman was briefly asked about whether he could identify other "mechanical contractors" that were on the same jobsites with him and his brother. That is insufficient to shift the burden. "Courts can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered." (Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 [failure of deponent to indicate that defendant contractor had been general contractor at site where he had been employed was insufficient to shift burden on defendant's motion for summary judgment, as he was not specifically asked about defendant]; see also Ganoe, 227 Cal.App.4th at 1584-1585 [evidence that decedent's coworker did not recall name of insulation contractor did not satisfy contractor's burden on summary judgment motion].) Thus, Defendant fails to show Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain the requisite evidence to prove causation. (See Ganoe, 227 Cal.App.4th at 1584-1585 [reversing summary judgment for defendant where plaintiffs' amended discovery responses cited to specific facts linking defendant to the decedent's exposure to asbestos, including identifying decedent's coworker as a product identification witness]; see also Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1442 [observing that a "motion for summary judgment is not a mechanism for rewarding limited discovery"].) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 4 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT ANDERSON, ROWE & BUCKLEY, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND NOTICE OF MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Defendant's attempt to show that Plaintiffs' responses to discovery are factually devoid falls short. Defendant argues that Plaintiffs make identical allegations in response to Defendant's Special Interrogatory No. 1, seeking "all facts" related to the cause of action for Negligence II-Survival. (Defense Exhibit D at 2:7-9.) In response to Special Interrogatory No. 1, Plaintiffs allege Defendant distributed, removed, or replaced unidentified asbestos containing products in close proximity to Decedent, causing exposure at unknown locations. (UMF No. 4; Motion, at p. 2.) However, as noted in Plaintiffs' opposition, Defendant failed to provide the Court with Plaintiffs' full responses. Defendant's exhibits to Special Interrogatory No. 1 only include pages 2 and 3, where the response is cut off mid-sentence. (See Defense Exhibits E, F and G at 2:3-28.) Plaintiffs also failed to provide the Court with the complete response to Special Interrogatory No. 1 in their opposition. It is impossible for the Court to evaluate the partial response to determine whether it is factually devoid. Thus, Defendant did not demonstrate by way of material facts included in the separate statement that Plaintiffs do not have, and cannot reasonably obtain, evidence of causation. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1442-1443; Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal. App. 4th 96, 105.) Defendant also fails to address Plaintiffs' responses identifying Robert Cantley (deceased), as a person with personal knowledge. (See Defense Exhibit E, F, G at 2:19; 33:21-24; and 33.) In regard to documents related to Decedent's exposure, Plaintiffs identified the deposition transcript of Mr. Cantley, taken in Jamie Mulvanny Miller v. Certainteed Corporation, et al, San Francisco Superior Court Case No. CGC-22-275872. (Defense Exhibits E, F, G at 32:19; 33:21-24.) In its moving papers, Defendant failed to address Mr. Cantley's testimony and/or its admissibility. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 3 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT PIERCE LATHING CO. (DBA PIERCE ENTERPRISES) MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 4 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Finally, although Defendant's motion is brought on the alternative ground that "Decedent's employer engaged in superseding conduct that intervenes in the chain of causation and destroys the element of causation as to Defendant" (Motion, at p. 2), Defendant presents no evidence or argument on that issue in its moving papers, and therefore must be deemed to have abandoned it. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 304 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on Friday, February 14, 2025, and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on February 18, 2025, at 9:00 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. =(304/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT PIERCE LATHING CO. (DBA PIERCE ENTERPRISES) MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 3 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Thus, unlike Andrews v. Foster-Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, Plaintiffs' interrogatory responses do provide specific facts known to Plaintiffs regarding Decedent's exposure to Defendant's products at specific jobsites. As a result, Defendant did not carry its initial burden on summary judgment. (See Ganoe, 227 Cal.App.4th at 1584-1585 [reversing summary judgment for defendant where plaintiffs' amended discovery responses cited to specific facts linking defendant to the decedent's exposure to asbestos, including identifying decedent's coworker as a product identification witness].) Defendant does not address any of that evidence, and makes no showing that it deposed or attempted to depose Mr. McCollum. "A motion for summary judgment is not a mechanism for rewarding limited discovery; it is a mechanism allowing the early disposition of cases where there is no reason to believe that a party will be able to prove its case." (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1442 [reversing summary judgment where defendant "produced no evidence allowing an inference that plaintiffs neither possess, nor reasonably can obtain, any evidence that [decedent] was exposed to an asbestos-containing product manufactured or supplied by [defendant]"]; see also Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 ["[c]ourts can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered"].) In the absence of evidence that Mr. McCollum will be unable to testify that Decedent was exposed to asbestos-containing products supplied by Defendant, the Court cannot find that Plaintiffs will be unable to establish causation as to Defendant. In addition, Plaintiffs also identified Defendant itself as a product identification witness, and Defendant failed to address the knowledge it possesses. Defendant therefore failed to shift the burden to Plaintiffs, and its motion for summary judgment must be denied on that ground alone. Defendant also contends that according to Decedent's death certificate he died in South Africa of "natural causes." As the opposition notes, Defendant seems to be arguing that Decedent did not have an asbestos caused death because his death certificate states that he died of natural causes. (Opposition, p. 9.) However, Defendant's motion offers no argument on this issue and instead focuses on the claims of exposure and product/identification. Regardless, Plaintiffs filed an expert declaration from Dr. Richard Cohen, in which he opines that the Decedent's death was contributed to by his two separate asbestos-caused diseases, asbestos and mesothelioma. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit A at 1-80.) Thus, Defendant fails to foreclose the possibility that Plaintiffs may yet prove asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant. (See Strobel v. Johnson & Johnson (2021) 70 Cal.App.5th 796, 802-803, 811-828.) (Tentative ruling continues in Part 4 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD HARDEMAN ET AL VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT CAHILL CONTRACTORS, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 4) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry) Defendant argues Plaintiffs cannot prove threshold exposure, based on its contention of factually devoid discovery responses, or alternatively, that Plaintiffs have no evidence that that Defendant acted with a disregard for Decedent's safety or that any office or director ratified any such conduct. (Motion, at p. 2,7.) Defendant's arguments are not well taken as its separate statement is deficient. Defendant relies on citations to evidence that are inaccurate and do not correspond to the referenced exhibit. Specifically, in Undisputed Material Facts ("UMF") Nos. 4, 8, 9, 12, 18, 19, 21 (incorrectly numbered), 23 and 24, Defendant cites to Exhibit G, Plaintiff's Responses to Defendant's Special Interrogatories, attached to the Declaration of Mr. Bugatto. The attached exhibit, which is dated November 7, 2023, consists of only eight pages, excluding the proof of service, and consists entirely of objections. However, as noted in Plaintiffs' opposition, on November 10, 2023, Plaintiffs amended and served comprehensive responses that were 100 pages in length. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit B.) The amended responses were served more than a year before the instant motion was filed. (Opposition, at p. 1.) Defendant failed to attach the amended responses to the instant motion. Defendant's moving papers cites to pages 10 through 95 of Exhibit G. These citations are inaccurate and do not support the asserted material facts. Ttentative ruling continues in Part 3 of 4)
